blocks.content: Every Spark executor's memory is divided into four regions. Understanding this model is essential for diagnosing OOMs and tuning cluster configuration.

# Executor memory layout:
# в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
# в”Ӯ  spark.executor.memory = 8g                         в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Reserved Memory (300 MB, fixed by Spark)     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  вҶҗ Spark internals, not configurable
# в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Usable = (8g - 300MB) = 7.7g                 в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Unified Memory Pool = 7.7g Г— 0.6 = 4.6g в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  вҶҗ spark.memory.fraction default 0.6
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Execution    в”Ӯ Storage            в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  вҶҗ share this pool dynamically
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (shuffle,    в”Ӯ (cached RDDs/DFs)  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ sort, agg)   в”Ӯ                    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
# в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  User Memory = 7.7g Г— 0.4 = 3.1g             в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  вҶҗ UDFs, Python workers, temp vars
# в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
# в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

rows: Python UDF workers, user variables, temp objects

sections.title: Spill: When Execution Memory Is Full

blocks.content: When more data needs to be sorted/shuffled than fits in execution memory, Spark spills to disk. Spill doesn't cause failures вҖ” it slows jobs dramatically (disk I/O is 100x slower than RAM) and can fill disks.

# Spill detection
# Spark UI вҶ’ Stage вҶ’ Tasks вҶ’ 'Shuffle Spill (Memory)' and 'Shuffle Spill (Disk)' columns
# If spill > 0 for many tasks вҶ’ memory pressure

# Common causes of spill:
# 1. Shuffle partitions too small вҶ’ each partition too large for memory
#    Fix: increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions

# 2. executor.memory too small for the data volume
#    Fix: increase spark.executor.memory or reduce partition size

# 3. Complex aggregation (collect_list) accumulating large in-memory state
#    Fix: rethink the aggregation approach

# Configuration to reduce spill:
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '400')  # more partitions вҶ’ smaller per-task data
spark.conf.set('spark.executor.memory', '16g')          # more RAM
spark.conf.set('spark.memory.fraction', '0.7')          # give more to execution pool

rows: Each partition too large for execution memory
collect(), toPandas(), show(100000) вҖ” pulling too much data to driver
Avoid collect() on large DFs; use .limit(N) or write to storage
Python worker (separate process) uses User Memory вҖ” large objects passed to UDF
Use Spark built-ins instead of Python UDFs; use Pandas UDFs (vectorized)
Too many DataFrames cached, evicting execution memory
Unpersist old caches; use DISK_ONLY or MEMORY_AND_DISK storage level

blocks.content:
# Cache storage levels вҖ” trade CPU for memory
from pyspark import StorageLevel

# Default вҖ” fast but uses most memory
df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)

# For large DataFrames that are read infrequently:
df.persist(StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY)  # no RAM used

# Serialized (compressed) вҖ” 2-5x less memory, 20% slower
df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER)

# Always unpersist when done
df.unpersist()

# Check what's cached
spark.catalog.clearCache()  # emergency: clear everything
Full GC pauses (JVM GC stops all threads) are a common source of mysterious slowdowns. Spark recommends G1GC for most workloads.

# Recommended GC configuration for Spark executors
spark.conf.set('spark.executor.extraJavaOptions',
    '-XX:+UseG1GC '
    '-XX:G1HeapRegionSize=32M '
    '-XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35 '
    '-XX:+PrintGCDetails '
    '-verbose:gc '
    '-XX:+PrintGCTimeStamps'
)
# G1GC is better than default ParallelGC for Spark's mixed-age object pattern
# G1HeapRegionSize: larger regions reduce GC boundary scanning overhead
# InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35: start GC earlier, avoid full GC

# Signs of GC pressure:
# - Spark UI: tasks show 'GC Time' > 10% of task time
# - Executor logs: 'Full GC' entries
# - Tasks appear stuck with no progress

Interview tips: (1) Python UDFs bypass Tungsten вҖ” they serialize data from JVM to Python, a 10x overhead. Always prefer Spark built-ins. (2) Pandas UDFs (vectorized) use Arrow for batch transfer вҖ” 10x faster than row-level Python UDFs. (3) Never do collect() in production without a .limit().

rune_cards.definition: Fraction of usable executor memory (after 300 MB reserved) allocated to the unified memory pool (execution + storage). Default 0.6. Increase to 0.7-0.8 if you see spill; decrease if Python UDFs OOM.

When execution memory fills up during sort/shuffle, Spark writes intermediate data to local disk (spill to disk). Job doesn't fail, but 100x slower. Visible in Spark UI Stage tasks as 'Shuffle Spill (Memory)' and 'Shuffle Spill (Disk)'.

Garbage-First collector вҖ” recommended JVM GC for Spark. Handles mixed-age objects well, reduces full GC pauses. Enable with -XX:+UseG1GC in executor extraJavaOptions. Better than default ParallelGC for most Spark workloads.

Vectorized Python UDF that transfers data in Arrow columnar batches instead of row-by-row. 10-100x faster than standard Python UDFs. Define with @pandas_udf decorator. Type hint determines return type and UDF type (scalar, grouped_map, grouped_agg).

quiz.question: A nightly job runs fine Monday-Thursday but OOMs on Friday. The Friday dataset is 3x larger. Logs show 'GC overhead limit exceeded'. What's the most targeted fix?
options: Increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to spread data across more tasks
Switch to Python UDFs to reduce JVM memory usage
quiz.feedback: More shuffle partitions = smaller partition size = less data each task holds in execution memory at once = less GC pressure. 'GC overhead limit exceeded' means the JVM is spending >98% of time in GC вҖ” usually because objects are too large relative to heap. More partitions + slightly more executor memory is the standard fix.

quiz.question: You have a Python UDF that processes each row. The job runs 10x slower than equivalent SQL. What's the first optimization to try?
options: Convert the Python UDF to a Pandas UDF (@pandas_udf) with vectorized batch processing
quiz.feedback: Standard Python UDFs serialize each row from JVM вҶ’ Python process вҶ’ back to JVM. Pandas UDFs use Apache Arrow for batch columnar transfer вҖ” 10-100x faster. Convert to @pandas_udf with appropriate return type. If possible, replace entirely with Spark built-in functions (no Python overhead at all).
